title: Smartphone Use During School Hours by US Youth in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
abstract: This study uses data on smartphone usage in schools from the ABCD (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development) Study to characterize smartphone app usage during school hours in a diverse sample of US adolescents.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data explicitly: 'EARS (Effortless Assessment Research System) app captures three weeks of passive sensing data on smartphone use in a subsample of adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study'

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone exposure measured via EARS passive sensing: 'smartphone use', 'app categories' (social, games, entertainment, communication, etc.); specifically measures time on Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat and other platform categories

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical test linking smartphone variable to non-SM outcome. Paper is descriptive analysis only: characterizes app usage during school hours with adjusted means (emmeans package) but no regression/correlation with external outcome variables

FT_IC4.evidence: Supplementary material indicates eMethods/eReferences present but main PDF shows descriptive statistics (means with 95% CI) for app usage but no coefficient, OR, or p-values for tested associations with outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Measurement/descriptive study without external analysis. Paper describes smartphone app usage patterns but reports no empirical analyses linking to behavioral/clinical/non-SM outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (JAMA, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC3 fails (no association test); this is a measurement/descriptive study, not an analysis with SM as outcome

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (N=640 with EARS passive sensing data from follow-up years 4, 5, 6)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone app use via EARS passive sensing; categorizes apps into 49 categories including social media (Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat) and games

FT_IC3.evidence: Supplementary material shows measurement methods and app categorization but main article text not fully accessible; unclear if reported associations to non-SM outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: Supplementary material references Table I with average times (e.g., medical category 0.13 min) and mentions means, but numeric coefficients/statistics for associations not visible in supplement reviewed

FT_IC5.evidence: Appears to be empirical analysis of ABCD Study smartphone use patterns but full methods/results not in supplementary material reviewed

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in JAMA (2026), full journal article (not preprint)

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Cannot fully assess directionality without access to main article results; supplementary material alone insufficient. If smartphone/SM use is examined as outcome, cannot determine if only demographic predictors with longitudinal design

title: Shared Genetic Risk in the Association of Screen Time With Psychiatric Problems in Children.
abstract: IMPORTANCE: Children's exposure to screen time has been associated with poor mental health outcomes, yet the role of genetic factors remains largely unknown. OBJECTIVE: To assess the extent of genetic confounding in the associations between screen time and attention problems or internalizing problems in preadolescent children. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This cohort study analyzed data obtained between 2016 and 2019 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at 21 sites in the US. The sample included children aged 9 to 11 years of genetically assigned European ancestry with self-reported screen time. Data were analyzed between November 2021 and September 2023. EXPOSURE: Child-reported daily screen time (in hours) was ascertained from questionnaires completed by the children at baseline. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: Child psychiatric problems, specifically attention and internalizing problems, were measured with the parent-completed Achenbach Child Behavior Checklist at the 1-year follow-up. Genetic sensitivity analyses model (Gsens) was used, which incorporated polygenic risk scores (PRSs) of both exposure and outcomes as well as either single-nucleotide variant (SNV; formerly single-nucleotide polymorphism)-based heritability or twin-based heritability to estimate genetic confounding. RESULTS: The 4262 children in the sample included 2269 males (53.2%) with a mean (SD) age of 9.9 (0.6) years. Child screen time was associated with attention problems (Î²â=â0.10 SD; 95% CI, 0.07-0.13 SD) and internalizing problems (Î²â=â0.03 SD; 95% CI, 0.003-0.06 SD). The television time PRS was associated with child screen time (Î²â=â0.18 SD; 95% CI, 0.14-0.23 SD), the attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder PRS was associated with attention problems (Î²â=â0.13 SD; 95% CI, 0.10-0.16 SD), and the depression PRS was associated with internalizing problems (Î²â=â0.10 SD; 95% CI, 0.07-0.13 SD). These PRSs were associated with cross-traits, suggesting genetic confounding. Estimates using PRSs and SNV-based heritability showed that genetic confounding accounted for most of the association between child screen time and attention problems and for 42.7% of the association between child screen time and internalizing problems. When PRSs and twin-based heritability estimates were used, genetic confounding fully explained both associations. CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: Results of this study suggest that genetic confounding may explain a substantial part of the associations between child screen time and psychiatric problems. Genetic confounding should be considered in sociobehavioral studies of modifiable factors for youth mental health.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 5.0, children aged 9-11 years (n=4,262) with genetically assigned European ancestry, 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Child-reported daily screen time measured from questionnaires at baseline (hours/day)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models with polygenic risk scores and heritability estimates; Gsens genetic confounding framework with structural equation models reporting β coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric reporting: β coefficients with 95% CI throughout (e.g., β=0.10 SD, 95% CI 0.07-0.13 SD for screen time-attention association; β=0.03 SD, 95% CI 0.003-0.06 SD for internalizing)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using genetic sensitivity analysis model (Gsens) with SNV-based and twin-based heritability frameworks

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA Network Open, November 2023

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Includes individual psychological/genetic predictors (ADHD PRS, depression PRS) as per criterion (b) violation; genetic factors are explicitly modeled as confounders/predictors alongside screen time

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'This cohort study analyzed data obtained between 2016 and 2019 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at 21 sites in the US'

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is generic 'child-reported daily screen time (in hours)' with no breakdown into phone-specific or social-media-specific components. No mention of social media, smartphone, texting, or app use separately measured.

FT_IC3.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC3 because FT_IC2 fails. Screen time exposure variable is not a qualifying phone/SM-specific measure.

FT_IC4.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC4 because FT_IC2 fails. While numeric values are reported (β=0.10 SD, β=0.03 SD), they are for non-qualifying generic screen time exposure.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cohort study with cross-sectional analysis at baseline, reports association between screen time and psychiatric outcomes with statistical tests (regression with genetic sensitivity analyses).

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in JAMA Network Open (2023); not a preprint, protocol, or duplicate.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply because FT_IC2 fails (generic screen time, not phone/SM-specific). Therefore paper is excluded before FT-EC7 assessment.

title: Role of Sleep and White Matter in the Link Between Screen Time and Depression in Childhood and Early Adolescence.
abstract: IMPORTANCE: With the widespread adoption of screen-based devices among adolescents, there is growing concern that more screen time could contribute to mental health problems such as depression. It is thus critical to identify potential mediating factors that could help explain this potential risk relationship. Recent evidence indicates that more screen time could impact sleep duration and brain structural connectivity (ie, white matter organization), which are critical for emotional health. Notably, sleep duration is a modifiable behavior that health care providers can easily target. OBJECTIVE: To identify the association between screen time during late childhood and depressive symptoms in early adolescence, and to investigate whether these associations are mediated by sleep duration and white matter organization. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This prospective study was conducted from January 2024 to June 2024. Data from the Adolescent Behavior Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study were used to identify clinical and neuroimaging characteristics of participants at late childhood (T1; defined as aged 9-10 years) and early adolescence (T2; defined as age 11-13 years). Children and their parent/caregiver were recruited across 21 US cities. Participants with no past/current psychiatric disorders at T1 were selected for analyses. Initial analyses were conducted in 2024 and finalized in February 2025. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURE: Outcomes included screen time assessed using a self-report questionnaire, sleep duration assessed using the Munich Chronotype Questionnaire, and depressive symptoms characterized using the Child Behavior Checklist. Neurite orientation dispersion and density imaging and a tract profile approach were used to characterize the orientation dispersion index of 3 white matter tracts that are known to be implicated with depression: cingulum bundle, forceps minor, and uncinate fasciculus. RESULTS: Analyses included 976 participants (460 children [47.1%] were female, mean [SD] age was 9.9 [0.6] years at T1 and 11.9 [0.6] years at T2). Each additional hour of daily screen time at T1 was associated with a 0.12-point (95% CI, 0.04-0.20; Pâ=â.008) increase in Child Behavior Checklist depressive score at T2. Shorter sleep duration and worse cingulum bundle organization (greater orientation dispersion index) at T2 mediated 36.4% (95% CI, 18.2%-63.6%) of the association between more screen time and more depressive symptoms. CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: Results of this study show that more screen time in late childhood was associated with more depressive symptoms, potentially due to shorter sleep and worse white matter organization during early adolescence. These findings emphasize the importance of promoting healthy habits and balancing screen time with adequate sleep.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states 'Data from the Adolescent Behavior Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study' and participants recruited across 21 US cities; explicitly ABCD data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured via 'self-report questionnaire' (abstract); specific smartphone/screen variable extracted as exposure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mediation analysis with inferential statistics: 'Each additional hour of daily screen time at T1 was associated with a 0.12-point (95% CI, 0.04-0.20; P=.008) increase in Child Behavior Checklist depressive score at T2.' Screen time predicts depressive symptoms (non-SM outcome).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients reported: β=0.12 (95% CI 0.04-0.20, p=.008) for screen time→depression association; mediation estimate 36.4% (95% CI 18.2%-63.6%).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective/longitudinal mediation study with baseline (T1, ages 9-10) and follow-up (T2, ages 11-13) data; reports phone/SM→non-SM outcome.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (JAMA Pediatrics, 2025); no evidence of duplicate/preprint status.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable (predictor of depression), not dependent variable. Paper reports screen time → depression association (non-SM outcome). FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract explicitly states: 'Data from the Adolescent Behavior Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study were used to identify clinical and neuroimaging characteristics of participants at late childhood (T1; defined as aged 9-10 years) and early adolescence (T2; defined as age 11-13 years). Children and their parent/caregiver were recruited across 21 US cities.' Uses U.S. ABCD Study data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract states: 'Outcomes included screen time assessed using a self-report questionnaire'. However, no PDF available and abstract provides no details on what specific smartphone/SM variable is measured. Generic 'screen time' without clarification of phone/SM specificity. Does not meet FT-IC2 requirement for a SPECIFIC smartphone OR social media variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Abstract reports: 'Each additional hour of daily screen time at T1 was associated with a 0.12-point (95% CI, 0.04-0.20; P = .008) increase in Child Behavior Checklist depressive score at T2.' Reports regression/inferential test (screen time → depression outcome).

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract provides extractable numerics: β coefficient of 0.12 (95% CI, 0.04-0.20, p=.008) for screen time → depression; mediation pathway statistics (36.4%, 95% CI, 18.2%-63.6%).

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective longitudinal study with mediation analysis examining associations between T1 screen time and T2 depressive symptoms, with sleep duration and white matter as mediators.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in JAMA Pediatrics 2025; no evidence of duplicate/preprint/protocol.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper reports screen time (exposure) → depression (non-SM outcome), not smartphone/SM as dependent variable. This is an IV→non-SM-outcome analysis, explicitly excluded by FT-EC7 rule.
